briefing.opening: Good morning, everyone. I will now begin the climate briefing for April 7, 2026.

sections.title: 🔴 Deepening Climate Crisis: Domestic and International Impacts and Warnings
sections.content: Domestically, a recent phenomenon has emerged where pine trees are turning autumn-colored and dying en masse due to climate change and pest attacks, revealing the vulnerability of ecosystems [1]. Internationally, climate change is altering major greenhouse gases in ways scientists did not anticipate [31], and research has also shown that many national parks are vulnerable to climate change [34]. Climate change is causing floods across continents worldwide [42], and rising sea levels are emerging as a health crisis, leading to growing calls for accountability from polluters [48]. Furthermore, widespread threats across human society are materializing, such as climate change and rising temperatures costing the average U.S. household $900 annually [25], and physicians explaining how it can affect diabetes patterns [43].

sections.title: 🇰🇷 Domestic Climate Response Efforts and the Dilemma of Energy Transition
sections.content: Domestically, swift actions to address climate change are being observed. Chungbuk Institute of Health and Environment has activated a mosquito surveillance system [2], Gyeongsangbuk-do is operating a carbon neutrality points system [3], and Mapo-gu has begun supporting flood prevention facilities in preparation for extreme weather [5]. Gwangmyeong City is also actively supporting manufacturers with costs for replacing eco-friendly and energy-efficient equipment [22], demonstrating vigorous efforts at the local government level. Meanwhile, while the share of renewable energy generation is expanding and grid innovation is needed [9, 17], the People Power Party warns that a renewable energy-only policy could lead to a 'Hormuz of the sky' and is advocating for a nuclear power-centric energy mix [7, 19, 21]. The Korea Federation for Environmental Movements is urging the abolition of policies that obscure the essence of renewable energy transition and social conflicts [10], while concerns are also being raised about the eco-friendliness of solar energy and policies that overly favor solar power [12, 13, 15, 18].

sections.title: 🌍 International Energy Security and the Risks of Fossil Fuel Dependence
sections.content: Internationally, wars are further highlighting the necessity of renewable energy transition and emphasizing the importance of energy security [8, 23]. Former UN Climate Change Executive Secretary Christiana Figueres warned that countries are 'held hostage' by their reliance on fossil fuels, describing the health impacts of climate change as 'the mother of all injustices' [46]. Disruptions in shipments of oil, gas, and fertilizers due to the Iran war serve as a reminder of the risks of the global economy's reliance on fossil fuels [49], and Chinese media has also reported on the potential for the Strait of Hormuz closure to cause chaos in global fossil fuel supplies [50]. In these circumstances, new approaches to energy security and carbon neutrality are urgently required [20].

sections.title: 🌱 Seeking Climate Solutions Through Technological Innovation and Collaboration
sections.content: Technological innovation and international cooperation efforts to address climate change are also active. Former U.S. Vice President Al Gore argued that artificial intelligence (AI) could be a solution for reducing carbon emissions [6], and indeed, AI is being researched as a way to help archaeologists cope with climate change [40]. California has launched new strategies to protect its agriculture and food sectors from climate change [41], and corporate eco-friendly transitions are continuing, such as Boehringer Ingelheim's Georgia site achieving carbon-neutral operations [45]. Additionally, Global Banking & Finance Review has nominated candidates for the best climate change initiatives of 2026 [38], and Carbon Brief has introduced a cohort of contributing editors comprising international academics [51], expanding the scope for knowledge sharing and collaboration.

sections.title: 📰 Widespread Impacts of Climate Change and Enhancing Social Awareness
sections.content: Climate change is impacting society and culture in various ways, extending beyond mere environmental issues. Discussions are underway on how climate change is reshaping forests [24], and research has also shown that it can give invasive plants powerful new advantages [36]. An interesting study suggesting that climate change could slow down Earth's rotation has also been introduced [33]. Stanford and New York Universities are hosting film events exploring climate change and mental health [26, 27], and a Lehigh University researcher is studying climate change using epiphytes [28], demonstrating active academic exploration. Efforts to raise awareness through art are also continuing, such as an artist in Kentville documenting climate change using yarn [44], contributing to deepening social dialogue and understanding of climate change [30].

briefing.closing: Today's briefing highlighted the severity of the climate crisis, diverse response efforts both domestically and internationally, and the complex discussions surrounding energy transition. Continued attention and action are essential for addressing climate change. This concludes today's climate briefing.

articles.original_title: Sea-level rise is a health crisis and we must hold polluters accountable | Christiana Figueres
articles.description: Those facing the earliest and harshest consequences are, overwhelmingly, those who did the least to create themWorld held hostage by reliance on fossil fuels, Christiana Figueres warnsWhat are the health impacts of sea-level rise, and who should pay?There are moments in history when a crisis long treated as distant reveals itself to be intimate, immediate and profoundly human. Sea-level rise is one of those moments.For years it has been discussed in the abstract language of centimetres, coastal

summary.phenomenon: Sea-level rise, long discussed as an abstract crisis, is now revealing itself as an intimate, immediate, and profoundly human crisis, specifically a health crisis.
summary.outlook: Those facing the earliest and harshest consequences of sea-level rise are overwhelmingly those who contributed the least to its causes.

articles.title: A new economic superpower could spark a global retreat from fossil fuels | Mark Hertsgaard and Kyle Pope
articles.description: Eighty-five countries have sought a roadmap to phasing out fossil fuels. A conference this month offers hope they could uniteThis article is published as part of the global journalism collaboration Covering Climate NowThe Iran war is also a climate war. Beyond its terrible human costs, the war’s disruptions of oil, gas, fertilizer and other shipments is another reminder of the risks inherent in basing the world economy on fossil fuels. The war’s jets, missiles and aircraft carriers, and the tank

summary.phenomenon: The Iran war is causing disruptions in shipments of oil, gas, and fertilizer, reminding the world of the risks inherent in basing the global economy on fossil fuels.
summary.cause: The global economy's reliance on fossil fuels means that events like the Iran war can trigger energy crises.
summary.outlook: Eighty-five countries are seeking a roadmap for phasing out fossil fuels, and this month's conference offers hope that they could unite.
